title: US Backs 278 AI Science Projects With $5 Billion - TCR 07/23/26
description:
The Genesis Mission committed $5 billion and 278 projects to AI-driven science across the national labs, AMD invested up to $5 billion in Anthropic and named it the first 2-gigawatt MI450 customer, and a new gene-editing method called KNIT inserted DNA fragments over 10 kilobases at up to 89% efficiency without double-strand breaks.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-23-2026/

0:00 Open and framing
0:28 Genesis Mission puts AI to work on science
6:08 Gene-editing stack advances at once
10:41 The compute race reaches $750 billion
15:01 AI redesigns evolution's starting point
18:27 AI-cited layoffs at Monday.com and Nine
21:52 Close

This episode covers the Genesis Mission's national-lab AI push, the gene-editing stack advancing across KNIT, ContactSeek, and a single-vector spinal muscular atrophy therapy, ProteinMPNN redesigning enzymes before evolution begins, the compute race reaching a $750 billion buildout target and Alphabet's first negative free cash flow in a decade, and AI-cited layoffs at Monday.com and Nine Entertainment.

The day's signal sits at the point where AI stops reading science and starts redrawing its starting conditions. The Genesis Mission wires the Department of Energy's supercomputing spine into research problems bottlenecked for decades - grid interconnection studies that take years, aimed at runs finishing in minutes. The same design-over-inheritance move scaled down to biology: KNIT knocks in large DNA payloads without severing both strands, ContactSeek reads AlphaFold3 contacts to cut off-target edits, a compact editor corrects spinal muscular atrophy in mice from one shot, and ProteinMPNN reshapes the fitness landscape before evolution runs. Underneath it all, AMD naming Anthropic its first 2-gigawatt customer opens a second silicon path to the frontier, even as OpenAI's buildout climbs to $750 billion and Alphabet goes cash-flow negative. Monday.com and Nine named AI while cutting staff, without the task-substitution evidence to prove it. The bottleneck keeps moving off human hours and onto compute, one field at a time.
